title: Ageing paper with coffee and tea
subtitle: Toning white paper to a warm parchment using cold-brewed coffee or strong tea
excerpt: Cold-brewed coffee and strong black tea both carry enough pigment to stain paper a convincing aged tone. The technique takes minutes per sheet and requires nothing beyond what is already in the kitchen.
sourceNotes: Coffee and tea staining of paper is a long-standing craft technique with no canonical pre-1928 source. The chemistry involved (tannins and gallic acid in tea; brown organic pigments in coffee) is documented in food-science literature rather than craft history. The practical method here is synthesised from widely documented contemporary craft practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A naturally occurring plant polyphenol that bonds to paper fibres and deposits a brown-yellow colour. Present in high concentration in black tea and coffee.
A coating applied to paper to alter its surface absorbency. Unsized paper (waterleaf) absorbs liquid readily; heavily sized paper resists it.
Grams per square metre: the standard measure of paper weight. Heavier paper (200 gsm and above) survives repeated wet applications better than lightweight stock.

content.text: Paper toned with cold coffee or strong tea reads convincingly as aged parchment. Coffee gives a warm brown. Tea gives a cooler, slightly yellow cast. Both are useful for aged-look envelopes, journal page inserts, and backgrounds for papercutting. The materials cost nothing beyond what is in the kitchen. Results are consistent once you know two rules: one coat per session, and never go back over wet paper.

items.name: Cold-brewed coffee or strong brewed black tea
items.substitutions: Instant coffee dissolved in cold water works; so do black, green, or rooibos tea, each producing a slightly different colour.

items.name: Cartridge paper, 220 gsm, or handmade cotton-linter sheets
items.substitutions: Cartridge paper at 130 gsm also works but wrinkles more on a single coat. Thin paper (below 90 gsm) often tears when wet.

items.name: Paste brush, 50 mm flat
items.substitutions: A kitchen pastry brush or a wide watercolour brush also works.

items.name: Weights for pressing flat
items.qty: 2-3 heavy books or a pressing board

content.text: Brew the coffee or tea at double strength and let it cool to room temperature. Do not use hot liquid: hot liquid warps lightweight paper more aggressively and can cause fibres to lift from the surface on older stock.
Lay the paper flat on baking paper. Dip the
or lightly sized side face-up. Load the brush with the brew and work across the surface in long, overlapping strokes in one direction. Cover the whole sheet in a single pass.
Do not go back over an area you have already brushed. Where wet meets wet, a dark line forms at the edge. This is the main source of streaks. Work confidently and keep moving.
Leave the sheet to dry face-up on the baking paper. Do not move it while wet: the paper is fragile when saturated, especially at
or below. Drying time is 15-30 minutes in a warm room.

Once dry, the sheet will have curled. Place it face-down on a flat surface, lay a piece of baking paper over it, and press with heavy books for at least two hours. Longer is better: overnight under a book press gives a reliably flat sheet.

A single coat gives a light, warm cream. For a deeper, more saturated tone, let the sheet dry completely and press flat, then apply a second coat in the same way. Two coats is the practical maximum for writing paper: a third coat makes the fibres noticeably more fragile and the surface prone to smearing when written on with a dip pen.

in black tea produce a cooler, more yellow-brown tone: closer to parchment. Coffee gives a warmer, redder brown. Rooibos tea (caffeine-free, red in colour) gives a soft terracotta. Each brew behaves differently depending on how strong it is and on the specific paper. Run a test strip before committing a full sheet.

items.cause: Going back over a wet area, or picking up the brush mid-stroke and restarting in the same place
items.fix: Apply a single pass, front to back, without lifting or returning the brush. Accept slight unevenness: an even antique tone is less authentic-looking than a slightly mottled one.

items.problem: Sheet tears when lifting off the baking paper
items.cause: Paper below 90 gsm becomes very fragile when wet. Lifting too soon.
items.fix: Use heavier paper (220 gsm or above) for this technique. If using lightweight stock, leave it on the baking paper until fully dry before touching it.

items.cause: Brew was too weak, or the paper was heavily sized and rejected the liquid
items.fix: Double the brew strength, or apply a second coat after the first has dried and been pressed flat. Heavily sized paper (hot-press watercolour, glossy card) resists water-based toning and is the wrong substrate for this technique.

items.cause: Not enough weight, or pressed before fully dry
items.fix: Ensure the sheet is bone dry before pressing. Use three or four heavy books, or a dedicated pressing board with a weight on top. Press overnight.

content.text: Toned paper works well as a calligraphy background: iron-gall ink reads clearly against a warm cream ground. It also suits papercutting backgrounds and insert pages in a hand-bound journal. The tonal variation between pages adds visual interest. Toned sheets work as endpapers in a pamphlet-stitch or long-stitch binding. Use 220 gsm or above for the folded endpaper role.
